items.passage: Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems on Earth, hosting thousands of marine species within a small area. Each reef forms slowly as colonies of tiny animals called polyps secrete hard calcium skeletons over centuries. These skeletons build into large frameworks that shelter fish and countless other creatures. Reefs also protect coastlines from strong waves, absorbing much of the wave energy before it reaches shore. However, warming seas can cause corals to expel the algae that feed them, leaving many reefs under threat.

items.passage: Sustainable agriculture seeks to produce food while preserving the long-term health of soil, water, and surrounding ecosystems. Farmers rotate crops between seasons so that different plants draw nutrients from varying soil depths. Cover crops are also planted to prevent erosion and to return organic matter back into the ground. Reducing chemical fertilizer use, meanwhile, helps protect nearby streams from harmful runoff. Although these methods often require more labor, they tend to keep farmland productive for future generations.

items.passage: Habit formation is the gradual process by which repeated behaviors become almost automatic responses to everyday cues. Each time an action is repeated in a stable context, the brain strengthens the pathway linking cue and response. After a while, the pathway becomes nearly automatic, requiring far less conscious attention than before. Rewards play an important role, since behaviors followed by pleasant outcomes are more likely to be repeated. However, breaking an established habit can be difficult, because the old neural pathway does not simply disappear.

items.passage: Roman engineers built an extensive network of roads, bridges, and aqueducts that connected distant regions of their empire. Aqueducts carried fresh water across valleys using a slight, continuous downward slope that relied purely on gravity. Roads were paved with fitted stones and layered gravel, allowing armies and merchants to travel quickly. Concrete, strengthened with volcanic ash, let builders construct domes and harbors that still stand today. Although many structures have crumbled, several Roman bridges and roads remain in use across modern Europe.

items.passage: Fossils are the preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms, offering a window into life from millions of years ago. Most fossils form when an organism is buried quickly by sediment before it can decay completely. Minerals gradually seep into the buried remains, replacing organic material with dense, rock-like layers. Because this process is rare, only a tiny fraction of past organisms ever became fossils. Scientists study these remains to reconstruct ancient environments and to trace how species changed over time.

items.passage: Comets are icy bodies left over from the early formation of the solar system, orbiting the sun on long paths. As a comet approaches the sun, heat causes its frozen surface to release gas and dust into space. This escaping material forms a glowing cloud around the nucleus, along with a long, bright tail. The tail always points away from the sun, pushed outward by solar wind and radiation pressure. Although most comets pass by only once, some remain trapped in orbits that return every few years.
